question_title: Will OPEC or OPEC+ officially announce an emergency production increase of at least 1.0 million barrels per day, beyond already scheduled adjustments, before May 1, 2026?
summary: OPEC+ is extremely unlikely to announce an emergency production increase of ≥1.0 mbpd before May 1, 2026. The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed since late February due to the US-Iran war, physically preventing key Gulf producers from exporting additional oil. The most recent OPEC+ meeting (April 5, 2026 — yesterday) resulted in only a symbolic 206k bpd "paper" increase for May that won't translate to real barrels. The 1.0 mbpd threshold is nearly 5x the most aggressive scenarios analysts discussed (400-500k bpd). Infrastructure damage, stranded spare capacity, OPEC's incremental culture, and the absence of a regular meeting before May 1 all strongly favor NO. The only path to YES requires Hormuz reopening + emergency session + 1.0 mbpd agreement in ~25 days — each step individually improbable.

factors.description: April 5 OPEC+ meeting just occurred and only agreed to a symbolic 206k bpd paper increase for May — nowhere near 1.0 mbpd emergency increase
Strait of Hormuz effectively closed since late Feb — key Gulf producers physically cannot export additional oil, making a 1.0 mbpd increase undeliverable
Gulf infrastructure has suffered severe damage from missile/drone attacks; officials estimate months to restore even if war stops
OPEC+ spare capacity extremely limited — only Saudi and UAE had meaningful spare capacity pre-crisis, now largely stranded
Next regular OPEC+ meeting ~May 3, after the May 1 resolution deadline; would need a special emergency session
OPEC culture is incremental — even the most 'aggressive' scenario discussed was 400-500k bpd, well below the 1.0 mbpd threshold
Enormous political/economic pressure from oil at $110+ and worst crisis since 1970s could motivate extraordinary action
If Strait of Hormuz partially reopens before May 1, OPEC+ could call an emergency meeting for accelerated supply increase
factors.conditional: If Strait of Hormuz reopens before late April

reasoning:
**Forecast: 4% probability (NO)**

The evidence strongly points against OPEC/OPEC+ announcing an emergency production increase of ≥1.0 million bpd before May 1, 2026:

- **Just met yesterday (April 5)**: OPEC+ only agreed to a symbolic 206k bpd "paper" increase for May — not even close to 1.0 mbpd
- **Physical impossibility**: The Strait of Hormuz has been closed since late February. Gulf producers can't export additional oil even if they increase quotas
- **Infrastructure destroyed**: Severe damage from the Iran conflict means months to restore capacity
- **Threshold too high**: 1.0 mbpd is ~5x the most aggressive scenario discussed (400-500k bpd)
- **No regular meeting before deadline**: Next scheduled session is ~May 3, after the May 1 cutoff
- **OPEC wouldn't announce what it can't deliver**: Credibility matters to the cartel

The only path to YES requires a ceasefire, Hormuz reopening, an emergency OPEC session, AND agreement on 1.0+ mbpd — all within ~25 days. Each step is individually improbable.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will OPEC or OPEC+ officially announce an emergency production increase of at least 1.0 million barrels per day, beyond already scheduled adjustments, before May 1, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43057)
[OPEC+ debates making oil output hike amid Iran war paralysis, sources say](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/05/opec-debates-making-oil-output-hike-amid-iran-war-paralysis-reuters.html)

[Opec+ approves ‘in principle’ May output increase amid war-related supply disruptions | The National](https://www.thenationalnews.com/business/energy/2026/04/05/opec-does-not-raise-output-levels-as-iran-conflict-keeps-oil-markets-on-edge/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if OPEC's official press room at https://www.opec.org/opec_web/en/press_room.htm publishes a statement before May 1, 2026 announcing an emergency production increase of at least 1.0 million barrels per day above previously scheduled supply changes. Otherwise, this question resolves to no.
fine_print: Count only a new announced increase beyond the pre-existing schedule. Mere speculation, draft communiqués, or unofficial minister comments do not count. Candidate Sources: Reuters energy coverage https://www.reuters.com/markets/commodities/
